dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems.
Hi sir, my name is Alex and I'm an EMS provider. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having abdominal pain for most of the day and trouble breathing in the last hour or so.
I see. On the call, we were dispatched for breathing problems, but you've mainly been experiencing epigastric pain, correct?

Yes, the breathing is related. I have COPD and it's been a little harder than normal, but the main issue is the pain in my stomach area-like a knife pushing through it. I haven't been able to sleep all night trying to get relief. It doesn't go away or change with movement. Well, it's better with rest, but the pain is still here and tiring. I'm dizzy, too, but it's been going on for a while now. It comes and goes with no notice. I was afraid it might be a heart issue, so I called 911 just in case.
Is your name Tom?
Yeah, it's Tom.
Tom, can you tell me your current date?
It's 2020 today, I believe.
Tom, what's the name of the street we're on?
We're on Seventh Avenue, I think.
And just to confirm, how many are we here? Just you and me?
Yeah, and the paramedic. Makes three of us.
Great-you are alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Your GCS is 15.
Sir, we'll do a few things all at once. First, I want to make sure your airway is clear and functioning. Can you take a few deep breaths for me? Inhale deeply and exhale slowly.
You look stable, and your breathing is not labored or noisy. I can hear clear breath sounds from both sides. No signs of airway obstruction noted at this time.

Tom's respirations are 18 per minute; they appear regular and mildly labored, but not in acute distress. He doesn't look pale or sweaty and there's no evidence of cyanosis. He's handling the effort, but it's a little more work than average for him, probably because of the COPD.

Pulse is 97, strong and regular. Blood pressure is 190/98. He doesn't demonstrate any signs of a pulse that's weak or thready. Skin temperature is normal. Oximetry is at 96. We will repeat at intervals as per protocol.
Tom, I want to know a bit more about what's going on today. You mentioned you have COPD. Are there any other conditions or medical issues we should be aware of?
Yeah, I had a heart attack a few years ago, but I was pretty lucky and it didn't leave anything worse behind. Otherwise, it's mostly my lungs, and I always carry a rescue inhaler with me for that.
You said the pain is sharp and stabbing. Where exactly in your abdomen is this pain located?
Mostly in the upper abdomen, right underneath my sternum-epigastric. It's near my heart area, or maybe just irritation, I don't know, but it's definitely there and won't subside no matter what I do.
When did the abdominal pain first start today?
It started earlier today, maybe in the early hours. It steadily became more of a problem, and each hour of movement only made the pain build up.
I understand. On a scale of 1 to 10, 10 being the worst you've ever had, how would you rate your worst pain?
I would say it's a 5. Not 10, but definitely enough to make me uncomfortable.
You've mentioned that dizziness has been ongoing. Does it come and go with no warning?
Yes, the dizziness is something I've been dealing with for a while now. It comes and goes with no warning and is not directly related to the abdominal pain or breathing, as far as I can tell.
What we saw earlier is that your skin does not show signs of pallor or sweating. Let's now take a closer look.
Your skin appears normal with no bruising, rashes, scars, or bite marks. No signs of jaundice or abnormal turgor.
Are you experiencing any pain or discomfort in your neck or head?
No, I'm fine up there.

Let's recheck the vitals. Pulse is still 97. Respirations at 18. Blood pressure is 190/98. SpO₂ is 96 and heart rate is regular. Everything remains consistent with our initial findings. No indication of immediate concern at this time.
I'm going to attempt to measure your blood glucose now.
We were not able to obtain a blood glucose reading due to resource limitations at this time.
We're going to take a 4-lead EKG to monitor your cardiac rhythm and compare to the 12-lead we'll get next. It's for our safety and to provide accurate data for the hospital.

We just completed the 4-lead EKG, and the result is consistent with sinus rhythm with occasional PVCs. Next, I'll do the 12-lead to confirm. If you have any questions about these procedures, feel free to ask.
The 12-lead EKG was performed and returned findings: abnormal with moderate ST depression. Let me document the results so we can include them in the report to the hospital.
Tom, we encountered some difficulty establishing IV access. We were not able to obtain access at this time. We may proceed with oral medications or prompt transport as needed.
Now, we're going to check your back, sides, and pelvis for any signs of pain or instability. Would you like to roll onto your side or can we gently lift your shirt for inspection?
You can go ahead and check. I feel okay, and it's better than not being checked properly.
No visible signs of pain or instability in the vertebral column or pelvis. You can remain on your back. No discomfort noticed during the exam.
Moving to your stomach now. I'll check for any mass or tenderness quickly without discomfort.
Go ahead. If it hurts I'll let you know.
No distension or obvious guarding observed in the epigastric area, as reported. You appear to be tender there.
Let's just double-check the vitals. Pulse remains at 97, respirations at 18, blood pressure 190/98. SpO₂ is still at 96. No change, so we remain confident in the current condition and monitoring.
Tom, you indicated you'd like to be transported to [COMPANY_NAME]. Do you still want to go there, and are you agreeable to us placing you on the stretcher and securing you for transport?
Yes, I do, and I'm comfortable with the plan.
We'll now move you with a stand and pivot transfer and secure you with straps and rails. The move will be smooth, and we'll keep you comfortable throughout.
You're secured. Let's start the transport. Everyone stay within the unit for safety and low vibration.
We'll constantly monitor your vitals during transport and ask a few more questions if needed. Is there anything else you want to tell us before en route?
No, I think that's all. Thank you for everything and the attention. I hope for the best.
We're en route now and monitoring you continuously. I will review your vitals one more before our arrival and adjust as needed.
We have followed the medical abdominal pain protocol, protocol 3-2, to ensure the best care is provided to you while we transport to the hospital.
